skill.name: seedance-v2
skill.description: Generate cinematic short-form video with ByteDance Seedance 2.0 Pro on RunComfy. Documents Seedance 2.0 Pro's strengths (multi-modal references — up to 9 images, 3 videos, 3 audio — synchronized in-pass audio with natural lip-sync, cinematic motion refinement), the 4–15s duration schema, and when to route to HappyHorse 1.0 / Wan 2.7 / Kling instead. Calls `runcomfy run bytedance/seedance-v2/pro` through the local RunComfy CLI. Triggers on "seedance", "seedance 2", "seedance v2", "seedance pro", "bytedance video", or any explicit ask to generate video with this model.
skill.summary: Generate cinematic short-form video with ByteDance Seedance 2.0 Pro on RunComfy via RunComfy CLI
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: Evaluated 89 static findings across 182 lines. All pattern matches (external_commands, network, filesystem) are false positives caused by the scanner misinterpreting markdown syntax and documentation examples. The skill is a legitimate video generation wrapper that calls the RunComfy CLI with hardcoded strings. Explicit security documentation confirms HTTPS transmission, no shell injection surface, proper token storage (0600), and file size limits (2 GiB). No confirmed security risks.

low_findings.title: Markdown code block syntax flagged as command execution
low_findings.description: The static scanner detected 67 instances of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' in markdown code blocks. These are legitimate code examples showing CLI usage patterns. The actual command invocations use hardcoded strings, not user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged locations are markdown fenced code blocks containing example CLI commands with hardcoded strings. No user input injection vector exists in the actual code.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs flagged as hardcoded network targets
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged URLs in documentation as potential exfiltration targets. These are legitimate documentation links and example media URLs provided by the user.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs refer to runcomfy.com homepage, documentation, and example media placeholders. The skill explicitly documents that outbound endpoints are limited to model-api.runcomfy.net.

content.user_title: Generate cinematic video with Seedance 2.0 Pro
content.value_statement: Creating professional short-form video with lip-sync and multi-modal references requires chaining multiple tools and managing complex input schemas. This skill wraps the RunComfy CLI to provide Claude with direct access to ByteDance Seedance 2.0 Pro, handling API calls, polling, and file downloads seamlessly.
actual_capabilities: Generate 4-15 second cinematic video clips with text prompts
Apply character consistency using up to 9 reference images
Add natural lip-sync synchronized to generated or reference audio
Incorporate scene continuity from up to 3 reference video clips
Control aspect ratio, duration, and resolution for various platforms
Download generated videos directly to a specified output directory
limitations: Maximum clip duration is 15 seconds per call
Output resolution capped at 720p on the playground variant
Reference videos and audio must be 2-15 seconds; audio files under 15MB
Lip-sync quality depends on prompt clarity and may vary

use_cases.title: Create brand-consistent spokesperson ads
use_cases.description: Marketing teams can generate consistent spokesperson videos by providing a character image reference. The AI maintains face and costume identity across multiple clips while adapting the narrative through text prompts.

use_cases.title: Produce multi-language narrative content
use_cases.description: Video producers can maintain visual identity while changing the audio track. Upload reference audio for voice tone and generate localized versions with the same visual consistency.

use_cases.title: Generate cinematic previs for film projects
use_cases.description: Filmmakers can quickly generate pre-visualization clips combining camera direction, lighting style, and character references to communicate shot ideas before full production.

prompt_templates.title: Simple text-to-video
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a short video of a barista explaining today's special in a friendly tone. Medium close-up, slow push-in, warm side light, soft cafe ambience.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner users who want to generate video from a text description only

prompt_templates.title: Character-consistent dialogue
prompt_templates.prompt: The same person as image 1 stands in a softly-lit recording booth, leaning into the mic. Says: 'We just shipped the biggest update of the year.' Calm conversational tone. Medium close-up, locked tripod, shallow depth of field.
prompt_templates.scenario: Users who need consistent character identity across scenes

prompt_templates.title: Lip-synced advertisement
prompt_templates.prompt: Medium close-up. The woman explains today's special in a warm friendly tone, slow push-in, soft window light, gentle cafe ambience. Use image reference for character consistency.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-modal cinematic shot
prompt_templates.prompt: Subject from image 1 walks through the cafe from video 1. Voice tone matches audio 1 reference. Cinematic push-in, warm natural lighting, film grain texture.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced users combining multiple reference inputs for complex cinematic shots

output_examples.input: Generate a 6-second video of a chef in a bright kitchen plating a dish. Warm lighting, medium shot, professional food photography style.
output_examples.output: Downloads a 720p MP4 video showing a chef in a well-lit kitchen carefully plating a gourmet dish, with warm natural lighting and shallow depth of field.

output_examples.input: A person from the reference image gives a presentation in a modern office. Confident tone, standing at a whiteboard. Business professional.
output_examples.output: Downloads a video featuring the reference character in a modern office setting presenting at a whiteboard, with professional lighting and confident body language.

best_practices: Place stable identity elements (faces, costumes, logos) in image_url references rather than describing them in text prompts to avoid drift
Match aspect ratio of reference media to your desired output to prevent unintended crops or scaling
Use camera direction keywords like 'medium close-up', 'slow push-in', or 'locked tripod' to get consistent cinematic results
anti_patterns: Mixing radically different aesthetic references (watercolor style with photoreal) causes identity confusion in the model
Describing a character's face in verbose detail in the text prompt instead of using an image reference
Requesting clips longer than 15 seconds which will trigger a 422 error from the API

faq.question: How do I maintain consistent character appearance across multiple videos?
faq.answer: Provide a character image via the image_url parameter. The model uses this as a reference while generating motion and dialogue. Avoid describing the character in text to prevent drift.

faq.question: What is the difference between generate_audio and audio_url?
faq.answer: generate_audio: true creates new synchronized speech and ambient sound during generation. audio_url references an existing audio track for voice tone guidance without requiring lip-sync to match it.

faq.question: Why is my video getting cropped unexpectedly?
faq.answer: This usually happens when the aspect ratio of your reference media does not match your output aspect_ratio setting. Ensure reference images and videos match your target format.

faq.question: Can I create videos longer than 15 seconds?
faq.answer: No, the maximum duration is 15 seconds per API call. For longer content, generate multiple clips and stitch them together in post-production.

faq.question: How do I set up the RunComfy CLI for CI environments?
faq.answer: Set the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable instead of running 'runcomfy login'. This avoids interactive authentication and works in containerized environments.

faq.question: What happens if a download exceeds the size limit?
faq.answer: The CLI aborts any single download larger than 2 GiB to prevent disk-fill attacks. If you need very high resolution outputs, ensure you have sufficient disk space.
